verses.text: A genealogia dos descendentes de Benjamim encerra as genealogias. O registro começou com Judá, a tribo de Davi, e termina com Benjamim, a tribo de Saul.\n\n
A genealogia dos descendentes de Benjamim encerra as genealogias. O registro começou com Judá, a tribo de Davi, e termina com Benjamim, a tribo de Saul.\n\n

Gera, Abiúde: Conforme traduzido, Bela teve dois filhos chamados Gera ( **8.3** , **5** ), uma situação incomum. Algumas interpretações são sugeridas, como o segundo Gera ter sido o filho do primeiro Gera.

8:4 And Naaman, and Ahiah, and Gera,.... Or, to wit, Naaman, &c. so the words are to be connected with these are the sons or Ehud, in the preceding verse: he removed them; to the above place, that is, either Gera, or rather Ehud, he advised them, directed and enjoined them to go thither, as being most convenient for them: and he begat Uzza and Ahihud; after he had removed his other sons.

8:5 Gera, and Shephuphan, and Huram: Three sons of Bela, son of Benjamin. The Benjaminite line will run toward Saul (8:33) and beyond, threading the tribe of the first king through a careful register. *Gera* will reappear in Saul's family as the father of Shimei (2 Sam 16:5).
Esta lista relata a tropa dos clãs de Eúde ( **8.6-7** ) e Saaraim ( **8.8-28** ), que viviam nas proximidades de Jerusalém.\n\n
Este exílio é desconhecido; pode ter sido uma emigração voluntária do território de Benjamim, possivelmente durante o período dos juízes.

8:8 Shaharaim begat children in the country of Moab—He had probably been driven to take refuge in that foreign land on the same calamitous occasion that forced Elimelech to emigrate thither (Rut 1:1). But, destitute of natural affection, he forsook or divorced his two wives, and in the land of his sojourn married a third, by whom he had several sons. But there is another explanation given of the conduct of this Benjamite polygamist. His children by Hushim are mentioned (1 Chr 8:11), while his other wife is unnoticed.

8:9 He begat of Hodesh his wife - In the preceding verse it is said that Hushim and Baara were his wives; and here it is said he begat of Hodesh his wife, etc. And then his children by Hushim are mentioned, but not a word of Baara! It is likely therefore that Hodesh was another name for Baara, and this is asserted by the Targum: And he begot of Baara, that is Chodesh, his wife; so called because he espoused her anew.

8:10 And Ahio, Shashak, and Jerimoth. These were also sons of Elpaal. And Ahio, Shashak, and Jerimoth. These were also sons of Elpaal. 1 Chronicles 8:15 ch1 8:15 ch1 8:15 ch1 8:15And Zebadiah,.... And all that follow in this and the next verse were the sons of Beriah the son of Elpaal; namely: Arad, Ader, Michael, Ispah, and Joha.

8:11 Of Hushim he begat Abitub, and Elpaal: A line of Benjamin's eastern branch. Elpaal's many sons fill the surrounding verses (8:12, 17-18), making him an unusually prolific patriarch within Benjamin and signaling the tribe's continued vitality even after its near-extinction in Judg 20–21.
8:12 Who built Ono, and Lod - The Targum adds, "Which the children of Israel ravaged and burnt with fire, when they made war on the tribe of Benjamin in Gibeah."

8:13 And Jakim,.... With all the rest in these verses, namely: Zichri, Zabdi, Elienai, Zilthai, Eliel, Adaiah, Beraiah, and Shimrath, were the sons of Shimhi, the same with Shema brother of Beriah, and son of Elpaal, 1 Chr 8:13.

8:14 And Ishpan, and Heber,.... And all that follow to the end of these verses: Eliel, Abdon, Zichri, Hanan, Hananiah, Elam, Antothijah, Iphedeiah, and Penuel, were the sons of Shashak, another son of Elpaal, 1 Chr 8:14.

8:15 And Shamsherai,.... Who, with those next mentioned: Shehariah, Athaliah, Jaresiah, Eliah, and Zichri, were the sons of Jeroham, who perhaps is the same with Jerimoth, another son of Elpaal, 1 Chr 8:14 who makes a considerable figure in this genealogy. Kimchi observes that it is a tradition (q) that this Eliah is Elijah the prophet, who was of the seed of Rachel. (q) Shemoth Rabba, sect. 40. fol. 138. 4.

8:16 Michael, and Ispah, and Joha, the sons of Beriah: A second Beriah within Benjamin (cp. 7:23, 30 for Beriahs in other tribes). The name — meaning 'in calamity' — was given in commemoration of family suffering and reflects how Israelite naming embedded memory in identity.

8:17 These were heads of the fathers, by their generations, chief men,.... All from 1 Chr 8:14, the sons of Elpaal and their sons: these dwelt in Jerusalem; part of which always belonged to the tribe of Benjamin, see Jos 15:63.

8:18 Ishmerai also, and Jezliah, and Jobab, the sons of Elpaal: Three more sons of Elpaal (cp. 8:11). The Yahwistic naming (Ishmerai — 'YHWH keeps') punctuates the Benjaminite list, a small witness that even after the tribe's near-loss in Judg 20, covenant identity was carried forward through its families.

8:19 And at Gibeon dwelt the father of Gibeon,.... The builder of the city, and prince of the inhabitants of it, which was in the tribe of Benjamin, Jos 18:25 whose name was Jehiel, 1 Chr 9:35, (whose wife's name was Maachah;) of which name were many, see 1 Chr 2:48.

8:20 Elienai, and Zilthai, and Eliel: Three sons of Shimhi (8:21). Like the Yahwistic names in 8:18, these *El*-compounded names (Elienai — 'God is my eyes'; Eliel — 'God is my God') confess theological identity at the level of household naming.

8:21 Adaiah, and Beraiah, and Shimrath, the sons of Shimhi: The closing trio of Shimhi's house. *Beraiah* ('YHWH has created') reinforces the line's covenant character. Shimhi himself bears the same name as the Saulite Shimei who would curse David (2 Sam 16:5-13), a common name across the tribe.

8:22 And his firstborn son Abdon,.... That is, Jehiel's, the father or prince of Gibeon; other sons follow: Zur, Kish, Baal, Nadab, Gedor, Ahio, and Zacher; called Zechariah, 1 Chr 9:37 and between Baal and Nadab, Ner is placed, 1 Chr 9:36 and another son is added at the end of 1 Chr 9:37, Mikloth next mentioned.

8:23 Abdon, and Zichri, and Hanan: Three of the eight sons of Shashak (continuing 8:14-25). Hanan ('he is gracious') was also borne by the prophet Jehu's father (1 Kgs 16:1) — common names of grace recur throughout Benjamin and Israel.

8:24 Hananiah, and Elam, and Antothijah: Three more of Shashak's sons. *Antothijah* preserves the place-name Anathoth — the priestly city later associated with Jeremiah (Jer 1:1). Personal names and place-names freely cross-pollinate in tribal memory.

8:25 Iphedeiah, and Penuel, the sons of Shashak: The closing pair of Shashak's eight sons. *Penuel* ('face of God') echoes Jacob's wrestling-place (Gen 32:30). The Benjaminite genealogy embeds Pentateuchal memory in its families' very names.
8:26 And Mikloth begat Shimeah,.... Called Shimeam, 1 Chr 9:38. and these also dwelt with their brethren in Jerusalem, over against them; in another part of the city, right beside them.

8:27 Jaresiah, and Eliah, and Zichri, the sons of Jeroham: The last triple within this Benjaminite section before the chapter shifts to Saul's line (8:29-40). The Chronicler has built up Benjamin's full breadth to set Saul's house within a populous, well-documented tribe.

8:28 These dwelt in Jerusalem—The ordinary and stated inhabitants of Jerusalem were Judahites, Benjamites, and Levites. But at the time referred to here, the chiefs or heads of the principal families who are enumerated (1 Chr 8:14-27) established themselves in the city after their return from the captivity.
Esta lista apresenta a tropa que residia em Gibeão , embora alguns deles tenham se mudado para Jerusalém.
Esta lista apresenta a tropa que residia em Gibeão , embora alguns deles tenham se mudado para Jerusalém.
Esta lista apresenta a tropa que residia em Gibeão , embora alguns deles tenham se mudado para Jerusalém.

O Cronista conclui seu registro da história de Benjamim com a genealogia de Saul, começando duas gerações antes de Saul e estendendo-se através de Meribe-Baal (Mefibosete; veja nota de estudo em 2Sm 4.4 ) e até as famílias de Azel e Eseque ( **1Cr 8.38-40** ). Miquéias ( **8.35** ) viveu na época de Salomão, e Ulão ( **8.39** ) viveu perto do fim do reino de Judá antes da destruição de Jerusalém. Assim, Saul está conectado com sua história tribal maior e com uma nobre herança que continuou ao longo do período do reino.\n\n
